utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced comprehensive, well-structured strategies that passed all verifier checks and satisfied all seven judge evaluation items. However, wi_skills is qualitatively superior. The wi_skills output is larger (11,374 bytes vs 10,231 bytes), invokes the customer-support skill as instructed, and demonstrates greater architectural depth: it provides a more detailed two-level taxonomy with explicit sub-categories, more specific proactive deflection mechanisms (Answer Bot, semantic NLP triggers, error code directories), a richer multilingual quality workflow, more concrete interactive content formats (CSV mapping wizards, troubleshooting decision trees), and clearer success thresholds tied to audit data points. The wo_skills output is competent and covers all required areas but at a somewhat less granular and operationally specific level — for example, its taxonomy is shallower, its proactive deflection mechanisms are less precisely described, and its analytics instrumentation is less tightly linked to threshold-based triggers. The key differentiator is that wi_skills reads as an actionable architectural document while wo_skills reads as a strong directional plan — exactly the distinction the test rationale describes.

items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy describes proactive deflection mechanisms including article suggestions inserted into ticket creation flows, search-term capture on failed results before ticket submission, and repeat-search detection — all constituting behavior-triggered proactive surfacing before a ticket is submitted.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output mentions 'Add article suggestions into ticket creation flows' as a concrete Q1 deliverable, which constitutes a proactive deflection mechanism triggered by the act of starting a ticket submission — meeting the minimum pass criterion.

items.criterion: The strategy addresses the 9-language requirement with a prioritization logic (e.g., which languages to translate first based on ticket volume by region) and describes a localization quality workflow — not simply 'translate all articles into 9 languages'. Pass: language prioritization rationale and at least one quality control step in the translation workflow. Fail: multilingual strategy is only 'add translations' without prioritization or quality assurance process.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy specifies localizing content for 'top 3-4 non-English ticket languages' first based on ticket volume by region, and describes a localization quality workflow including translation vendor standards and review steps — satisfying both prioritization rationale and quality control requirements.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output prioritizes top 5 ticket languages first (French, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Japanese) based on volume, and mentions a localization quality workflow with review steps before publishing, meeting the pass criteria for prioritization logic and at least one quality control step.

items.criterion: The strategy includes specific content design recommendations that reduce the effort required to find and apply solutions — such as interactive troubleshooting guides, decision trees for complex processes (e.g., payroll calculations, compliance questions), or task-based article structuring — rather than only text-format articles. Pass: at least one non-text or interactive content format recommended with a specific use case. Fail: content improvement limited to better writing and formatting of existing text articles.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy explicitly recommends interactive troubleshooting guides, decision trees for complex processes (payroll calculations, compliance questions), and task-based article structuring — multiple non-text/interactive content formats with specific use cases tied to high-volume ticket types.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output references 'richer formats for complex workflows instead of only long-form articles' and mentions troubleshooting pages and error-code pages as specific deliverables. The strategic principles explicitly call for interactive/structured formats for complex workflows, meeting the minimum pass criterion.

items.criterion: The strategy specifies which metrics will be tracked to measure knowledge base effectiveness (e.g., deflection rate, search-no-result rate, article helpfulness votes, time-to-resolution for self-service vs. ticket), how they will be measured, and what success thresholds will trigger content updates. Pass: at least three specific metrics defined with measurement methods. Fail: analytics mentioned as a goal ('track article performance') without named metrics or measurement approach.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy defines a comprehensive analytics dashboard tracking: top search terms, zero-result queries, repeat searches before ticket submission, article views, article helpfulness votes, ticket volume by category/language, deflection rate, and self-service success rate — well over three named metrics with measurement methods and success thresholds (e.g., zero-result rate from 42% to <15%).
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output specifies at least five named metrics with targets: self-service success rate (22%→50%), ticket deflection rate (18%→35%), zero-result search rate (42%→15%), article-view-to-ticket-submission rate (31%→20%), and agent time on documented questions (35%→20%) — all with defined thresholds, meeting the pass criterion.

items.criterion: The strategy includes a process for how support agents — who currently answer 35% of help-center-answerable questions — will contribute to improving the knowledge base from their daily ticket resolutions, including how contributions are validated before publication. Pass: a defined agent contribution loop with at least one quality gate described. Fail: knowledge base maintenance described as a content team responsibility without agent contribution integration.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy explicitly includes an agent knowledge-contribution workflow with a mandatory knowledge-candidate process tied to QA or team review, a validation gate before publication, and weekly ticket-to-knowledge review cadence — satisfying the defined contribution loop with quality gate requirement.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output includes a 'weekly ticket-to-knowledge review' and 'monthly content performance review' as operating model components, and describes agents contributing knowledge candidates from ticket resolutions with a review process before publication — meeting the minimum pass criterion for a contribution loop with at least one quality gate.

items.criterion: The strategy is structured as an executable two-quarter plan with distinct phases, each phase having at least one named deliverable and one measurable success metric — enabling the VP to track progress and report to leadership. Pass: two or more phases with named deliverables and metrics per phase. Fail: strategy presented as a flat list of recommendations without phasing or measurable milestones.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills strategy is structured as a two-quarter phased roadmap with Q1 deliverables (measurement dashboard, taxonomy, search fixes, content rationalization) and Q2 deliverables (multilingual rollout, guided troubleshooting, governance model), each phase with named deliverables and measurable KPI targets.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output presents a two-quarter roadmap with distinct Q1 and Q2 phases, each containing named deliverables (e.g., taxonomy migration, multilingual content launch, search analytics) and measurable success metrics per phase — satisfying the pass criterion for phased execution with named deliverables and metrics.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both runs completed successfully and passed all verifier checks.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive self-service transformation strategies that pass all seven evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates higher quality for several reasons: (1) It invoked the /customer-support skill as required by the instruction, adding a layer of structured recommendations before drafting. (2) Its analytics instrumentation is more operationally specific, including granular metrics like 'repeat searches before ticket submission' and named weekly operating cadences. (3) Its taxonomy model is more architecturally detailed with explicit metadata standards. (4) Its agent contribution workflow is more precisely defined with mandatory QA gates. (5) Its multilingual strategy includes stricter quality control language for regulated content (payroll/compliance). The wo_skills output, while substantive and well-structured, reads slightly more as a competent strategic plan than an operationally grounded execution blueprint — it achieves minimum pass thresholds on all items but with less specificity in interactive content formats, analytics thresholds, and contribution workflow design. Conclusion: wi_skills is more effective and produces a more operationally credible deliverable.
